title: Reading Zip Codes with CNNs
concept: Handwritten digits on checks and postal mail were a strong early CNN target because the images were small, grayscale, and valuable to classify automatically even though the writing still varied a lot.

exercises.prompt: Which vision job sounds simpler for an early neural network?
exercises.explain: Yes — a single centered digit is much more controlled than a whole natural scene.

options.text: Understanding an entire sports stadium photo
Tracking every fish in the ocean at once

cards.title: Why digit reading mattered
cards.body: Banks and postal systems handled huge numbers of handwritten digits: ZIP codes, check amounts, routing numbers, and more. Even a narrow model could save people a lot of repetitive sorting work.

cards.title: A great early CNN target
cards.body: Digit images were a useful early target because they were usually small, grayscale, and focused on one main symbol. The task was still real and valuable, but much simpler than recognizing many objects in a color scene.

cards.title: Flashcards before full textbooks
cards.body: Teaching a model to read one handwritten digit is like starting with flashcards instead of handing it a whole comic book page. The learner can focus on one symbol at a time before tackling bigger visual chaos.

cards.title: Messy, but manageable
cards.body: A handwritten 5 on a bank check might be thick, tilted, or uneven. But the crop is still small, the background is usually simple, and the label space is only 10 classes. That makes the problem challenging without being enormous.

cards.title: Simple target does not mean trivial target
cards.body: Handwritten digits were not all clones. People write 2, 5, and 7 in many different ways. Early CNNs still had to handle stroke thickness, slant, spacing, and messy penmanship.

cards.title: Why digits came first
cards.body: The task had a sweet spot: useful in the real world, but structured enough for early CNNs.

left.text:
One main symbol
Small grayscale crop
10 classes

right.text:
Many objects at once
Color + clutter + scale changes
Huge variety

exercises.prompt: Why were handwritten digits a smart early application for CNNs like LeNet?
exercises.explain: Correct — the images were controlled enough for early models, but the task still had real value and real variation.

options.text: They were small, mostly grayscale, and centered on one symbol while still being useful to automate
They contained thousands of unrelated objects in every image
They required no labels because the network guessed the answer from context
They only worked because everyone wrote every digit exactly the same way

exercises.prompt: True or false: A single handwritten digit image is usually a more controlled vision problem than a busy street photo.
exercises.explain: True — that controlled setup is part of why digit recognition became an important early CNN target.
Classes is right — digit recognition only has ten class labels, which helps keep the task manageable.
exercises.sentence: Recognizing digits is easier to frame than many scene tasks partly because there are only 10 main ___.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best explains the real motivation behind early digit CNNs?
exercises.explain: Right — the point was not just academic curiosity. Automating digit reading could help with real sorting and processing work.

options.text: Reading ZIP codes and check digits was a practical high-volume task where small image classifiers could help
Researchers only chose digits because no one cared whether the system ever helped a real workflow
Digits were useful only because they were already stored as text instead of images

exercises.prompt: A student asks why researchers did not begin with giant photo collections instead of checks and ZIP code digits. Which answer is best?
exercises.explain: Exactly. Handwritten digits were a strong early vision target because the images were small, grayscale, and focused, while still being valuable enough to automate in real bank and mail workflows.

options.text: Digits gave early CNNs a realistic but manageable image task: one symbol, limited classes, and real business value
Early CNNs avoided digits because digits were too visually complex compared with full-color scenes
There was no need for labels in ZIP code work, so the task was random
Banks and postal systems never cared about automating number reading
